skill.name: structural-search
skill.description: Use ast-grep to find, review, and refactor code by syntax tree structure instead of plain text matches.
skill.summary: AST-based code search and refactoring guidance for ast-grep.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, script, network, and credential patterns, but review shows most are ast-grep search examples for finding risky code in user projects. The confirmed risk is operational: the skill is Bash-enabled and documents commands that can scan, rewrite, or fix many files when a user requests those actions.

medium_findings.title: Bash Execution for ast-grep Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access and instructs users to run ast-grep commands. This is expected for the skill, but user-controlled patterns and shell arguments should be reviewed before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly allows Bash and provides many shell commands. The commands are core functionality rather than malicious behavior, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: In-Place Rewrite and Fix Commands
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes ast-grep rewrite and fix commands that can modify project files. This is useful for refactoring, but it can cause broad unintended changes if run without preview or source control.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple documented commands apply replacements or fixes. The behavior is legitimate, but it directly affects the filesystem when users choose apply modes.

medium_findings.title: Shell Pipelines Can Process Many Files
medium_findings.description: Advanced examples combine ast-grep with fd, rg, jq, fzf, and xargs. These pipelines are normal developer workflows, but copied commands can run across many files or open tools based on command output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clearly documented as search workflows, but xargs and parallel execution increase the blast radius of mistakes or unsafe user-provided patterns.

low_findings.title: Dangerous API Strings Are Detection Patterns
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged eval, innerHTML, exec, spawn, subprocess, and pickle strings. Review shows these are ast-grep patterns for finding vulnerable code, not code executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings appear inside Markdown command examples for ast-grep searches. There is no evidence that the skill evaluates or imports those snippets as executable code.

low_findings.title: Network Indicators Are Search Examples or Placeholders
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged fetch patterns and a hardcoded URL. The fetch lines are search patterns, and the URL is an example reference in a YAML rule template.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context shows no network request made by the skill. The URL is example.com, and fetch appears only as a pattern to locate calls in user code.

low_findings.title: Credential Strings Are Search Patterns
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged API key and secret strings in the security reference. These lines define ast-grep patterns for finding hardcoded credentials in user repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential terms appear in a section named Secrets/Credentials and are used as search patterns. No evidence found that the skill reads environment variables or exfiltrates secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Bash access and ast-grep shell commands are required for the skill. Users should inspect generated commands before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct Bash access is declared and command examples are present. This is confirmed functionality, not hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Modification Through Rewrites
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents rewrite and fix modes that can change source files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented flags are intended to modify files. The impact depends on the user-approved command scope.

content.user_title: Find Code Patterns with AST Search
content.value_statement: Text search misses semantic code patterns and creates noisy results. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use ast-grep for precise structural searches and refactors.
actual_capabilities: Find function calls, imports, hooks, classes, and language-specific syntax patterns.
Provide ast-grep commands for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Go, and Rust.
Preview simple refactors with ast-grep replacement patterns.
Guide custom YAML rule creation for repeated code checks.
Show integration examples for pre-commit hooks, CI scans, and editor workflows.
limitations: Requires the ast-grep CLI to be installed in the user environment.
Search accuracy depends on the selected language and pattern syntax.
Rewrite examples need manual review before applying changes.
Does not replace full security testing or compiler-aware static analysis.

use_cases.title: Refactor Common API Usage
use_cases.description: Find repeated function calls or imports and prepare a controlled ast-grep replacement plan.

use_cases.title: Audit Security-Prone Patterns
use_cases.description: Search for risky constructs such as dynamic execution, raw HTML insertion, and unsafe deserialization.

use_cases.title: Create Team Code Rules
use_cases.description: Build reusable ast-grep YAML rules for team conventions, CI checks, and pre-commit workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Simple Function Call
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner search for one known call pattern.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use structural-search to find every call to logger.info in this repository. Show the ast-grep command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Search One Language Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ast-grep to find Python functions decorated with @app.route. Limit the search to the app directory and summarize the matches.

prompt_templates.title: Preview a Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Find console.log calls and prepare a preview replacement to logger.debug. Do not apply file changes until I approve.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Reusable Rule
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced YAML rule and workflow creation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an ast-grep YAML rule to detect React dangerouslySetInnerHTML usage. Include test cases and a CI command that fails on matches.

output_examples.input: Find all React useEffect hooks that use dependency arrays.
output_examples.output: A short ast-grep command, a list of matching files, and notes about effects that may need dependency review.

output_examples.input: Preview replacing var declarations with const declarations.
output_examples.output: A replacement plan that shows the search pattern, the replacement intent, and a reminder to review results before applying changes.

output_examples.input: Create a rule for blocking console.log in TypeScript.
output_examples.output: A reusable rule description, suggested severity, test examples, and commands for local and CI validation.

best_practices: Run search-only commands before any rewrite or fix command.
Limit searches by language, directory, or file type to reduce noise.
Review matches in source control before committing automated changes.
anti_patterns: Running broad rewrite commands without previewing the match set.
Using text regex when an AST pattern can express the syntax directly.
Treating ast-grep security matches as confirmed vulnerabilities without manual review.

faq.question: What is structural search?
faq.answer: Structural search matches code by syntax tree shape instead of plain text. It can find semantic patterns that regex often misses.

faq.question: Does this skill require ast-grep?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is guidance for the ast-grep CLI and assumes the sg command is available.

faq.question: Can it modify files?
faq.answer: Yes, when rewrite or fix commands are approved and run. Preview commands should be used first.

faq.question: Which languages are covered?
faq.answer: The references include JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Go, and Rust patterns, plus general rule examples.

faq.question: Is this a security scanner?
faq.answer: It can help find risky code patterns, but results need human review and should not replace dedicated security testing.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code when the local environment supports the required commands.
